reasoning:
1) Baseline: We are comparing future reserves against the most recent value available on 2025-05-11 (which corresponds to the May 7 reading of ~3,218 billion). This level is depressed because mid-April tax receipts shifted massive amounts of cash out of bank reserves and into the Treasury General Account (TGA). 
2) Short-term (May 18 & June 10): Under the reinstated debt ceiling, the Treasury cannot issue net new debt and is forced to spend down its TGA balance to fund government operations. As the Treasury spends this cash natively, it flows back into the banking system, increasing reserve balances. Historically and mechanically, reserves rebound strongly in May and June after the tax drain, pushing both dates highly likely to record an increase.
3) Medium-term (August 9): By early August, the government is expected to hit the "X-date" where extraordinary measures are exhausted. If unaddressed, the TGA will be near zero and reserves will peak. However, if Congress lifts the ceiling in late July (as requested by the Treasury), rapid issuance of T-bills will instantly begin refilling the TGA, aggressively draining reserves. This creates a bimodal uncertainty, bringing the probability closer to 0.5.
4) Long-term (Nov 7): By November, the debt ceiling will have been resolved and the TGA fully restored (a projected rebuild that drains billions from reserves). Paired with ongoing Quantitative Tightening (QT), reserves should be structurally lower than the May baseline. However, they will be approaching the Fed's Lowest Comfortable Level of Reserves (LCLoR) estimated around $3.1T–$3.2T, at which point the Fed is expected to halt QT or inject liquidity to defend the floor.
5) Per constraints, uncertainty strictly increases with horizon, smoothing probabilities toward 0.5.
